name: Feature Flag
description: Controls application behavior at runtime by evaluating named flags, enabling release, experiment, operations, and permission decisions without redeploying code.
when_to_use:
Separating feature release from code deployment
Trunk-based development with incomplete or latent code paths
Progressive rollout to cohorts, percentages, regions, tenants, plans, or beta users
A/B or multivariate experiments that need stable user assignment
Operational kill switches for fast degradation during incidents
Permissioning or entitlement checks that must evolve without widespread code changes
benefits:
Runtime control without redeployment
Reduced release risk through gradual exposure
Fast rollback or kill switch behavior
Safer continuous delivery and trunk-based development
Targeted enablement by user, group, tenant, plan, region, device, time window, or percentage
Experimentation and multivariate delivery
Clear separation between deployment, release, and business/operations decisions
drawbacks:
Stale flags create technical debt and cognitive load
Too many scattered conditionals make code hard to reason about
Incorrect defaults can expose unfinished or unsafe behavior
Flag evaluation infrastructure can become a runtime dependency
Combinatorial testing grows as flags interact
Long-lived flags require stronger ownership, naming, audit, and cleanup discipline
Client-side flags can leak intent and must not protect sensitive authorization alone
use_cases:
Release toggles for hiding incomplete functionality
Experiment toggles for A/B and multivariate testing
Ops toggles for disabling expensive or risky paths under load
Permissioning toggles for plans, roles, tenants, or beta programs
Progressive delivery by percentage or canary cohort
Dark launches and internal-only validation
Emergency rollback, graceful degradation, and circuit-breaker-like kill switches
structure: Define a Flag Store that owns flag definitions, defaults, variants, targeting rules, and metadata. Use a Flag Evaluator or Toggle Router to evaluate a flag key against an Evaluation Context. Keep business code behind named decision methods so feature decisions are decoupled from scattered conditionals. For short-lived release flags, a small conditional may be acceptable; for long-lived permission or experiment flags, prefer decision objects, dependency injection, strategy selection, or policy-like abstractions. Track owner, purpose, type, creation date, expiry date, and cleanup plan for every flag.
participants: Flag Definition: stable key, type, default value, variants, targeting rules, metadata, owner, and expiry policy
Flag Store: durable source of flag configuration, often external to the application deployment
Flag Evaluator or Toggle Router: resolves the flag value for a request or execution context
Evaluation Context: user, tenant, plan, region, device, environment, request attributes, or operational state used for targeting
Decision Object: domain-facing API that answers meaningful questions instead of exposing raw flag keys everywhere
Toggle Point: the code location where behavior changes based on an evaluated decision
Administration and Audit Surface: controlled interface for changing flags, recording who changed what and why
Cleanup Process: recurring review that removes expired flags and dead code paths
collaborations: Callers ask a decision object or evaluator for a typed value using a stable flag key and an evaluation context.
The evaluator combines default values, stored configuration, targeting rules, percentage rollout, and optional time windows to produce a deterministic decision.
The toggle point selects behavior, delegates to a strategy, injects an implementation, or suppresses a nonessential operation.
Telemetry records evaluations, variants, exposure events, and operational outcomes so rollouts and experiments can be judged safely.
Owners retire temporary flags once the rollout, migration, or experiment is complete.
consequences: Deployment and release become independent activities.
Behavior can change per request, per audience, or per environment without a new build.
The system gains a powerful operational control plane, so access control, audit, defaults, and failure modes become part of the design.
The pattern is safe only when paired with lifecycle management; unmanaged flags become hidden branches in production code.
Tests should cover default/off behavior, enabled behavior, targeting rules, fallback behavior, and important flag combinations.
implementation: Classify every flag before creating it: release, experiment, ops, or permissioning. Choose static, dynamic-by-configuration, or dynamic-by-context evaluation according to that classification. Store flags outside the code deployment when runtime control is required, but keep safe defaults in code for availability. Centralize decision logic in a feature-decision layer instead of spreading raw flag checks across the codebase. Use deterministic bucketing for percentage rollouts so the same actor receives a stable experience. Prefer server-side evaluation for security-sensitive decisions. Instrument exposures and outcomes. Give each temporary flag an owner and expiry date, then remove the flag and obsolete code path after rollout or experiment completion.

relationships.description: Runtime feature flags often store definitions and current states outside the deployed application.
Percentage and cohort targeting enable canary-style progressive exposure.
Blue-green switches infrastructure versions while feature flags switch application behavior inside a deployed version.
Operational flags can act as manual kill switches or graceful-degradation controls alongside automated circuit breakers.
Long-lived flags can select strategies instead of scattering conditionals through business code.
Feature flags can gradually redirect behavior during incremental migration.
